items.myth: Machine learning means the computer programs itself without human involvement.
items.reality: Humans choose the task, collect and label data, pick model families, define metrics, deploy models, and monitor failures. ML automates pattern discovery inside that frame—not the entire engineering loop.
items.example: A fraud model still needs analysts to define "fraud," label cases, and approve deployment thresholds.

items.myth: More training data always fixes a bad model.
items.reality: Data must be relevant, correctly labeled, and representative of production inputs. Bad labels, distribution shift, or wrong features limit gains from volume alone.
items.example: Adding millions of English emails does not help a Chinese spam filter if deployment traffic is Chinese.

items.myth: Machine learning and artificial intelligence are identical.
items.reality: AI is the broad goal of intelligent behavior; ML is one major approach—learning from data. Rule-based expert systems and classical search are AI without ML.
items.example: A chess engine using minimax search is AI; AlphaZero learning from self-play is AI via ML.

items.myth: A model that fits training data perfectly has learned the right pattern.
items.reality: Perfect training fit often signals overfitting—memorizing noise or ids instead of features that generalize. Generalization on held-out data is the real test.
items.example: A decision tree with unlimited depth reaches 100% train accuracy on a tiny dataset but fails on new patients.
